content.text: The crown jewel of elementary number theory.

content.title: Quadratic Reciprocity
content.content:
Let $p$ and $q$ be distinct odd primes. Then:
\[
\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\left(\frac{q}{p}\right) = (-1)^{\frac{p-1}{2} \cdot \frac{q-1}{2}}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using Quadratic Reciprocity
content.solution:
Since $7 \equiv 3 \pmod{4}$ and $13 \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, we have
\[
\left(\frac{7}{13}\right) = \left(\frac{13}{7}\right) = \left(\frac{6}{7}\right) 
= \left(\frac{2}{7}\right)\left(\frac{3}{7}\right).
\]

$7 \equiv -1 \pmod{8}$, so $\left(\frac{2}{7}\right) = 1$.

$\left(\frac{3}{7}\right) = \left(\frac{7}{3}\right)$ (since $7 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$, $3 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$, flip sign) $= -\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -1$.

Thus $\left(\frac{7}{13}\right) = 1 \cdot (-1) = -1$.

content.title: Jacobi Symbol
content.content:
For odd $n = p_1^{e_1} \cdots p_k^{e_k} > 1$ and any integer $a$:
\[
\left(\frac{a}{n}\right) = \left(\frac{a}{p_1}\right)^{e_1} \cdots \left(\frac{a}{p_k}\right)^{e_k}.
\]

content.text:
The Jacobi symbol extends quadratic reciprocity to composite moduli. 
It inherits the key properties:

**Warning:** $\left(\frac{a}{n}\right) = 1$ does *not* imply 
$a$ is a QR mod $n$ when $n$ is composite!
